advantages: Max-margin objective often generalizes well on small-to-medium tabular and text datasets with clear class separation.
Support-vector sparsity: prediction depends on a subset of training points, not the entire dataset like k-NN.
Convex optimization — single global optimum for the standard soft-margin formulation (no random local minima from the loss).
Effective in high-dimensional spaces (e.g., sparse text) where distance-based methods struggle with the curse of dimensionality.
Kernel extension (see Kernels) handles nonlinear boundaries while retaining margin-based theory.
disadvantages: Default decision scores are not calibrated probabilities — extra steps (Platt scaling) needed for probability interpretation.
Training cost scales poorly on very large datasets (roughly O(n²) to O(n³) for classic solvers; linearSVC is faster but still sensitive to sample size).
Sensitive to feature scaling and hyperparameter C (and kernel γ); poor tuning yields under- or over-fitting.
Plain linear SVM cannot model complex nonlinear structure without kernels or feature engineering.
Multiclass requires one-vs-rest or one-vs-one reductions — not as seamless as softmax logistic regression.
